section.title: God and Idols
section.sectionRationale: Set among oracles of coming judgment, this chapter pauses to mock the whole enterprise of idolatry, a carved log dressed in silver and unable even to walk, and to praise the LORD who alone made heaven and earth. The chapter ends with Jeremiah's own prayer, caught between confessing that no one directs his own steps and pleading for correction tempered by mercy.
section.thesis: The nations' idols are worthless, man made, and powerless, but the LORD is the true and living God, Maker of heaven and earth, so that fearing anything else, whether carved images or one's own strength, is folly, and the only wise path is to entrust oneself to Him even in judgment.
section.frameNote: Four movements: a mocking exposure of idols as worthless, man made, and lifeless; a doxology praising the LORD as the true and living God who made all things; a warning of coming exile pictured as a collapsing tent; and Jeremiah's own prayer, acknowledging that no one directs his own steps and asking for correction with justice, not wrath.

units.label: Do not learn the ways of the nations
units.note: idols cannot speak, walk, or harm

verses.text: Hear the word that the LORD speaks to you, O house of Israel.

This is what the LORD says: 
“Do not learn the ways of the nations or be terrified by the signs in the heavens, 
though the nations themselves are terrified by them.
For the customs of the peoples are worthless; they cut down a tree from the forest; it is shaped with a chisel by the hands of a craftsman.

They adorn it with silver and gold and fasten it with hammer and nails, 
so that it will not totter.
Like scarecrows in a cucumber patch, their {{elil|idols}} cannot speak. They must be carried because they cannot walk. Do not fear them, for they can do no harm, and neither can they do any good.”

units.label: There is none like You, O LORD

verses.text: There is none like You, O LORD. You are great, and Your name is mighty in power.

Who would not fear You, O King of nations? This is Your due. For among all the wise men of the nations, and in all their kingdoms, 
there is none like You.
But they are altogether senseless and foolish, instructed by worthless idols made of wood!
Hammered silver is brought from Tarshish, and gold from Uphaz, the work of a craftsman from the hands of a goldsmith. Their clothes are blue and purple, all fashioned by skilled workers.
But the LORD is the true God; He is the {{chai|living}} God and eternal King. The earth quakes at His wrath, and the nations cannot endure His indignation.
Thus you are to tell them: “These gods, who have made neither the heavens nor the earth, will perish from this earth and from under these heavens.”

The LORD made the earth by His power; He established the world by His wisdom 
and stretched out the heavens by His understanding.
When He thunders, the waters in the heavens roar; He causes the clouds to rise from the ends of the earth. He generates the lightning with the rain and brings forth the wind from His storehouses.
Every man is senseless and devoid of knowledge; every goldsmith is put to shame by his idols. For his molten images are a fraud, and there is no breath in them.
They are worthless, a work to be mocked. In the time of their punishment they will perish.
The Portion of Jacob is not like these, for He is the Maker of all things, and Israel is the tribe of His inheritance, the LORD of Hosts is His name.
Gather up your belongings from this land, you who live under siege.

For this is what the LORD says: 
“Behold, at this time I will sling out the inhabitants of the land and bring distress upon them so that they may be captured.”
Woe to me because of my brokenness; my wound is grievous! But I said, “This is truly my sickness, and I must bear it.”
My tent is destroyed, and all its ropes are snapped. My sons have departed from me and are no more. I have no one left to pitch my tent or set up my curtains.
For the shepherds have become senseless; they do not seek the LORD. Therefore they have not prospered, and all their flock is scattered.
Listen! The sound of a report is coming, a great commotion from the land to the north. The cities of Judah will be made a desolation, a haunt for jackals.

units.label: Correct me, O LORD, but only with justice

verses.text: I know, O LORD, that a man’s way is not his own; no one who walks directs his own steps.
Correct me, O LORD, but only with justice, not in Your anger, or You will bring me to nothing.

Pour out Your wrath on the nations that do not acknowledge You, and on the families that do not call on Your name. For they have devoured Jacob; they have consumed him and finished him off; 
they have devastated his homeland.

elil.gloss: Worthless things, a mocking wordplay on el, god, that reduces idols to mere nothings. The same root gives Scripture some of its sharpest satire against false gods, calling them elilim, no gods at all, rather than granting them any real power.

chai.gloss: Living, alive. The LORD is repeatedly called the living God in contrast to lifeless idols that must be carried because they cannot walk; this same word roots Israel's confession that her God actually acts, speaks, and reigns.

basic.context: In the middle of oracles announcing judgment, Jeremiah pauses to mock the idols the nations trust: logs cut from the forest, decorated with silver and gold, nailed down so they will not fall over, unable to speak, walk, or do any good or harm. Against this parade of helpless statues, Jeremiah bursts into praise: there is none like the LORD, who made the heavens and the earth by His own power and wisdom. The chapter turns personal at the end, as Jeremiah himself prays, asking for correction tempered by justice rather than raw anger.
basic.hermeneutics: This is both satire and doxology in the same breath, exposing the absurdity of idol worship, carrying an object so it will not fall down, while exalting the living God who needs no one to hold Him up. The chapter's final prayer shows the prophet applying his own message to himself, confessing human dependence and asking for merciful discipline.
basic.originalLanguages: Jeremiah calls the idols elilim, worthless things, a biting wordplay on el, god, that reduces them to nothings rather than real gods. By contrast the LORD is chai, living, alive, active, the one true God whose wrath makes the earth quake, unlike idols with no breath in them at all.
basic.typology: The contrast between dead idols that must be carried and the living God who carries His people points forward to the true and living Word, Jesus Christ, who is not carried by human hands but who Himself sustains all things and, unlike any idol, actually rose from the dead.
basic.christ: John opens his Gospel echoing this chapter's praise of the LORD who made the heavens and the earth, applying that same creative power to the Word who became flesh, Christ, through whom all things were made, John 1:1-3. Where idols have no breath in them, Jesus is the resurrection and the life, breathing life into the dead rather than needing to be carried.
basic.god: God alone made the earth by His power and established the world by His wisdom; no other so called god shares in that work, and none will endure it. He is also personal enough that Jeremiah can bring his own fears to Him directly, asking for correction shaped by justice and mercy rather than unrestrained wrath.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:1-3 credits Christ with the same creative power praised here. Isaiah 44:9-20 gives a parallel satire of idol making. Psalm 115:4-8 warns that those who make idols become like them. Acts 17:24-25 echoes this chapter's logic that the true God needs nothing from human hands.
basic.oneStory: This is Scripture's ongoing contrast between dead things people make and the living God who made everything else, a contrast that runs from Eden's true worship through the prophets' satire of idols to the Word made flesh who is Himself the true and living God among us.

context.literary: This chapter functions almost as an interlude within the wider indictment of 7:1-10:25, shifting from Judah's specific sins to a broader theological contrast between the LORD and the false gods of every nation, before returning to Judah's own coming exile in verses 17 to 25.
context.historical: Likely addressed to a Judah increasingly drawn to the astral and idol worship of surrounding nations, including Babylonian and Canaanite religious practices, even as the real threat of exile to those same nations was approaching.
context.canonical: This chapter stands in a long line of biblical idol satire alongside Isaiah 44 and Psalm 115, and its praise of the Creator anticipates the New Testament's own creation theology centered on Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the absurdity and powerlessness of idol worship in stark, almost comic detail, to exalt the LORD as the sole true and living Creator, and to apply that same trust personally, showing even the prophet depends wholly on God's mercy rather than his own strength.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical temptation toward idolatry among Judah's neighbors and, at times, Judah itself; it does not merely condemn ancient wood carving. The enduring, prescriptive truth is that anything trusted in place of the living God, whether an image, an ideology, or one's own strength, is equally worthless.

debates.question: Is verse 11, written in Aramaic rather than Hebrew, an authentic part of Jeremiah's original text?

views.label: An authentic, deliberately inserted Aramaic proverb
views.case: Many scholars note this verse may have functioned as a memorable proverb or slogan, perhaps circulated among exiles who spoke Aramaic, that Jeremiah or a later editor incorporated intentionally to be quotable across language communities.

views.label: A later scribal addition
views.case: Because it is the only Aramaic verse in an otherwise Hebrew chapter, some propose it was added by a later hand, though it appears in the earliest manuscript traditions and coheres fully with the chapter's message.

debates.bearing: Either way, the verse's content is fully consistent with the chapter's argument and is treated as authoritative Scripture in the manuscript tradition; the debate is about origin, not about its truthfulness or canonical status.

study.originalLanguages: Jeremiah names the idols elilim, worthless things, a biting wordplay on el, god, reducing them to nothings that only mimic real deity. Against them stands the LORD, described as chai, living, alive, the one whose wrath makes the earth quake, unlike lifeless images with no breath in them. The wordplay itself becomes an argument: no man made thing can share the name or the life of the living God.

typology.type: The LORD who made the heavens and the earth by His power and wisdom, Jeremiah 10:12
typology.fulfillment: John identifies Christ as the Word through whom all things were made, applying this same creative supremacy to Jesus, John 1:1-3.

typology.type: Idols with no breath in them, unable to walk or speak, Jeremiah 10:5, 14
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and living God, breathes resurrection life into the dead rather than needing to be carried or propped up, John 11:25.

study.christ: John's Gospel opens by echoing this chapter's praise of the LORD who made the heavens and the earth, crediting that same creative power to the Word who became flesh in Jesus Christ, John 1:1-3. Where idols have no breath in them and must be carried because they cannot walk, Jesus is the resurrection and the life, walking out of His own tomb and giving life to all who trust Him, the ultimate vindication of the living God this chapter praises against every dead alternative.
study.god: God alone made the earth by His power and established the world by His wisdom; no rival so called god shares in that work, and every one of them will perish. Yet He is also near enough that His own prophet brings his fears directly to Him, asking not to be spared discipline but for that discipline to be shaped by justice and mercy rather than unrestrained wrath, trusting God's character even under judgment.

crossReferences.note: Credits Christ with the same creative power this chapter ascribes to the LORD alone.
Gives an extended, similarly satirical exposure of idol making.
Warns that those who make idols become as lifeless as the idols they trust.
Paul echoes this chapter's logic, that the true God needs nothing from human hands, unlike man made idols.

study.oneStory: This is Scripture's ongoing contrast between dead things people make with their own hands and the living God who made everything else, a contrast that runs from the garden's true worship through the prophets' satire of idols to the Word made flesh, who is Himself the true and living God dwelling among His people.

sermon.title: Acceptable Worship, Part Two
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly on Jeremiah 10:1-16, showing that understanding the true object of worship, the living God as He has revealed Himself, is what distinguishes acceptable worship from the idolatry this chapter exposes.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah say idols are ultimately unable to do?

options.text: Speak, walk on their own, or do any harm or good.
options.note: Verse 5 lists all of these limitations plainly.

options.text: Be decorated with silver and gold.
options.note: The text describes idols as commonly decorated this way; that is not their limitation.

options.text: Be carved from wood by a craftsman.
options.note: This describes how idols are made, not a limitation being mocked.

options.text: Be transported from one region to another.
options.note: Close to the point about being carried, but the text's emphasis is on total powerlessness, not merely portability.

quiz.q: According to verses 12-13, what has the LORD done that no idol can do?

options.text: Made the earth by His power, established the world by wisdom, and commands the storehouses of wind and rain.
options.note: Verses 12-13 credit the LORD alone with creation and control of the weather.

options.text: Set up altars in every city of Judah.
options.note: That describes Judah's idolatrous practice, condemned elsewhere in Jeremiah, not the LORD's unique work here.

options.text: Been carved by a skilled goldsmith.
options.note: That describes how idols are made, the exact opposite of the LORD's uncreated nature.

options.text: Been imported from Tarshish and Uphaz.
options.note: That describes the materials used for idols, not an act of the LORD.

quiz.q: What title does verse 16 give to the LORD in contrast to the idols?

options.text: The Portion of Jacob, the Maker of all things, the LORD of Hosts.
options.note: Verse 16 names Him this way, directly opposed to the man made idols described earlier.

options.text: The craftsman of Tarshish.
options.note: This phrase describes idol makers, not the LORD.

options.text: The scarecrow of the nations.
options.note: That image is used mockingly of the idols themselves, not of the LORD.

options.text: The King of the goldsmiths.
options.note: The chapter does call Him King of nations, verse 7, but this specific title is not used; the actual title in verse 16 concerns Jacob's inheritance.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah describe the coming exile in verses 19-20?

options.text: As a tent destroyed with its ropes snapped and no one left to pitch it again.
options.note: Verse 20 uses this exact image of a collapsed, abandoned tent.

options.text: As a wedding feast interrupted by joyful news.
options.note: The imagery here is of devastation and loss, not celebration.

options.text: As a harvest gathered in abundance.
options.note: The image is one of loss and ruin, not abundance.

options.text: As a fortified city that withstands every siege.
options.note: The passage speaks of the land being besieged and its inhabitants scattered, not of successful resistance.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah ask for in his prayer in verses 23-24?

options.text: That the LORD correct him with justice rather than in anger, since no one directs his own steps.
options.note: Verses 23-24 record this exact humble request.

options.text: That he be spared from all discipline entirely.
options.note: Jeremiah does not ask to avoid correction, but asks that it come with justice, not destructive anger.

options.text: That God pour out wrath on Judah immediately.
options.note: That request appears in verse 25 concerning the nations, not as Jeremiah's personal plea in verses 23-24.

options.text: That his own wisdom be sufficient to direct his path.
options.note: Jeremiah actually confesses the opposite, that a man's way is not his own, verse 23.

questions: Jeremiah mocks idols that must be carried because they cannot walk. What things do you find yourself carrying, propping up, or anxiously maintaining that should instead be trusting you?
The LORD alone made the heavens and the earth by His power and wisdom. How does remembering this reshape your view of the pressures or fears you are carrying today?
Jeremiah confesses that no one who walks directs his own steps. Where are you tempted to act as though your life is fully in your own control?
Jeremiah asks to be corrected with justice, not in anger. How do you typically respond when God disciplines or corrects you, with trust in His character or with fear of His wrath?
John's Gospel echoes this chapter's praise of the Creator to describe Christ. How does seeing Jesus as the living God who made all things change the way you worship Him this week?

translationNotes.note: The image of scarecrows in a cucumber patch, or field, was a common ancient Near Eastern comparison for something propped up and harmless in appearance but utterly powerless in reality.
Tarshish and Uphaz are both associated with prized sources of precious metal in the ancient world; naming them underscores the costliness lavished on objects that remain, in the end, worthless.
This single verse is written in Aramaic rather than Hebrew, unusual within the chapter; it may have functioned as a memorable, quotable proverb for use even among Aramaic speaking exiles.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit and sermon link verified via live web fetch this session confirming Alistair Begg's sermon on Jeremiah 10:1-16 at truthforlife.org.
